Factoring polynomials is one of the most important Algebra I skills because it connects expressions, equations, and graphing. This hub groups the lesson content and the focused practice work in one place.
In this hub, you will:
Review the most common factoring mistakes students make Practice factoring out GCFs and factoring trinomials Recognize and factor difference of squares expressions
data.explore-this-hub#Explore This Hub: Start with 5 Common Mistakes When Factoring Trinomials for explanations and common pitfalls Open Practice Questions to work through the factoring sequence and a mixed miscellaneous set

data.4-ignoring-the-leading-coefficient-#4. I:
Factoring trinomials is a core algebra skill, but it is also where many students get stuck. Most errors aren’t caused by “not knowing the math,” but by missing small steps that make the problem much harder than it needs to be.
Here is the first and most frequent mistake students make.
1. Skipping the Greatest Common Factor (GCF) Students often try to factor the trinomial immediately without first checking for a common factor.
This makes the problem harder than it needs to be.
Example Factor: 3x2+15x+18 3x^2 + 15x + 18 3x2+15x+18❌ Common Wrong Attempt
A student tries to factor the trinomial directly: (3x+?)(x+?) (3x + ?)(x + ?) (3x+?)(x+?) Because the numbers are larger than necessary, it becomes harder to find the correct factors.
✅ Correct Solution
First check for a Greatest Common Factor (GCF).
Each term contains a factor of 3, so factor it out: 3x2+15x+18=3(x2+5x+6) 3x^2+15x+18 = 3(x^2 + 5x + 6) 3x2+15x+18=3(x2+5x+6)Now factor the trinomial inside the parentheses: x2+5x+6=(x+2)(x+3) x^2+5x+6=(x+2)(x+3) x2+5x+6=(x+2)(x+3) Final Answer: 3(x+2)(x+3) 3(x+2)(x+3) 3(x+2)(x+3) 💡 Tip
Always check for a Greatest Common Factor (GCF) before factoring a trinomial.
Factoring out the GCF simplifies the expression and makes the remaining trinomial easier to factor.
2. Mixing Up Positive and Negative Signs This mistake happens when a student finds the right numbers but assigns the plus or minus signs to the wrong binomial.
Example Factor: x2+x−6 x^2 + x - 6 x2+x−6❌ Common Wrong Attempt
A student finds the factors 333 and 222 but assigns the negative sign to the larger number: (x−3)(x+2) (x - 3)(x + 2) (x−3)(x+2)Check: −3+2=−1-3 + 2 = -1−3+2=−1. The middle term should be +1x+1x+1x.
✅ Correct Solution
The factors of −6-6−6 must add to +1+1+1. Since the sum is positive, the larger factor (333) must be positive: (x+3)(x−2) (x + 3)(x - 2) (x+3)(x−2) 💡 Tip
The “Add-Back” Test. Before moving on, mentally add your two numbers. If they don’t exactly match the middle coefficient (including the sign), your answer is wrong.
3. Matching Multiplication but Failing Addition Many students find a pair of factors that multiply to the constant ccc and assume they are finished without checking the sum.
Example Factor: x2+10x+24 x^2 + 10x + 24 x2+10x+24 ❌ Common Wrong Attempt
A student sees 242424 and immediately thinks of 12×212 \times 212×2: (x+12)(x+2) (x + 12)(x + 2) (x+12)(x+2)Check: While 12×2=2412 \times 2 = 2412×2=24, the sum 12+2=1412 + 2 = 1412+2=14. We need a sum of 101010.
✅ Correct Solution
List all factor pairs of 242424 until the sum matches 101010:
3×8=243 \times 8 = 243×8=24 (Sum: 111111)
4×6=244 \times 6 = 244×6=24 (Sum: 101010)
Final Answer: (x+4)(x+6)(x + 4)(x + 6)(x+4)(x+6)
💡 Tip
The Sum-Product Table. If you are stuck, draw a small table. Label one side “Product” and the other “Sum.” Don’t stop until both columns match your trinomial.
4. Ignoring the Leading Coefficient (a≠1a \neq 1a=1) Students often try to use the “simple” shortcut even when the x2x^2x2 term has a coefficient other than 111.
data.5-neglecting-to-verify-via-redistributio: The final mistake is psychological: students finish the work but don’t verify if the answer actually works.
data.conclusion-from-mistakes-to-mastery#Conc:
Factoring is more about discipline than raw talent. By checking for the GCF first, being careful with your signs, and always verifying with FOIL, you eliminate 90% of the reasons students lose points.
Ready to put these tips into action? The best way to stop making these mistakes is through repetition. Pick a topic from our curriculum and start a practice session to build your “factoring muscle.”
data.example-3#Example:
Factor: 2x2+7x+3 2x^2 + 7x + 3 2x2+7x+3❌ Common Wrong Attempt
A student ignores the 222 and factors it like a simple trinomial: (x+1)(x+3) (x + 1)(x + 3) (x+1)(x+3)Check: (x+1)(x+3)=x2+4x+3(x+1)(x+3) = x^2 + 4x + 3(x+1)(x+3)=x2+4x+3. This does not match the original 2x2+7x+32x^2 + 7x + 32x2+7x+3.
✅ Correct Solution
Use the AC Method. Multiply a×ca \times ca×c (2×3=62 \times 3 = 62×3=6). Find factors of 666 that add to 777 (666 and 111):
Split the middle: 2x2+6x+1x+32x^2 + 6x + 1x + 32x2+6x+1x+3
Factor by grouping: 2x(x+3)+1(x+3)2x(x + 3) + 1(x + 3)2x(x+3)+1(x+3)
Final Answer: (2x+1)(x+3)(2x + 1)(x + 3)(2x+1)(x+3)
💡 Tip
Look at the Front. Always look at the x2x^2x2 term first. If there is a coefficient, you cannot use the simple shortcut. You must use the AC method or trial and error.
data.example-4#Example:
A student factors x2+3x−10x^2 + 3x - 10x2+3x−10 and gets (x+5)(x−2)(x + 5)(x - 2)(x+5)(x−2).
❌ Common Mistake
Turning in the paper without a quick 5-second check. Even a small sign error can ruin the entire problem.
✅ Correct Solution
Perform a quick FOIL check (First, Outer, Inner, Last):
F: x⋅x=x2x \cdot x = x^2x⋅x=x2
O/I: −2x+5x=3x-2x + 5x = 3x−2x+5x=3x
L: 5×−2=−105 \times -2 = -105×−2=−10
The result x2+3x−10x^2 + 3x - 10x2+3x−10 matches the original problem.
💡 Tip
The 5-Second FOIL. You don’t always have to write it down. At the very least, mentally multiply the “Last” terms to check ccc and the “Outer/Inner” terms to check bbb. It takes 5 seconds and saves your grade.
